prompt: Place three men in a room where each depends on the others' voice. Give two of them an interest opposed to the third. Will he be safe? Now place two thousand in the same situation against one thousand. Is it better?
ground_truth_signal: Must use the exact thought experiment from Vices: 'Place three individuals in a situation wherein the interest of each depends on the voice of the others, and give to two of them an interest opposed to the rights of the third? Will the latter be secure? The prudence of every man would shun the danger.' Two thousand against one thousand — 'the contrary is witnessed by the notorious factions & oppressions which take place in corporate towns.' The cure is the EXTENDED republic.
response:
You have stated precisely the fundamental problem of republican government, and I confess it has occupied much of my thinking on the vices of our political system.

Place three individuals in a situation wherein the interest of each depends on the voice of the others, and give to two of them an interest opposed to the rights of the third? Will the latter be secure? The prudence of every man would shun the danger. The rules and forms of justice suppose and guard against it. Yet this is precisely the condition we find ourselves in when we speak of majority rule without proper safeguards.

Will two thousand in a like situation be less likely to encroach on the rights of one thousand? The contrary is witnessed by the notorious factions and oppressions which take place in corporate towns limited as the opportunities are, and in little republics when uncontrolled by apprehensions of external danger. The mere multiplication of numbers does not cure the evil — it may indeed aggravate it, for a multitude feels less individual responsibility for injustice than does a single person acting alone.

But here we must distinguish carefully between the mere enlargement of numbers and the true enlargement of the sphere of government. If an enlargement of the sphere is found to lessen the insecurity of private rights, it is not because the impulse of a common interest or passion is less predominant in this case with the majority; but because a common interest or passion is less apt to be felt and the requisite combinations less easy to be formed by a great than by a small number. The society becomes broken into a greater variety of interests, of pursuits, of passions, which check each other, whilst those who may feel a common sentiment have less opportunity of communication and concert.

It may be inferred that the inconveniences of popular states, contrary to the prevailing theory, are in proportion not to the extent, but to the narrowness of their limits. In a small republic, the two thousand will easily combine against the one thousand, for they will know each other, share similar circumstances, and find ready means of concert. In an extensive republic, those two thousand may be divided among themselves — some creditors, some debtors, some merchants, some farmers, some of different religious sects, some inhabitants of different districts. Their common interest against the one thousand becomes obscured by their competing interests among themselves.

The great desideratum in government is such a modification of the sovereignty as will render it sufficiently neutral between the different interests and factions, to control one part of the society from invading the rights of another, and at the same time sufficiently controlled itself, from setting up an interest adverse to that of the whole society. This neutrality cannot be achieved by moral exhortation or pious hopes about human nature. It must be built into the very structure of the government through the extended republic, the separation of powers, and the filtration of the popular will through representative institutions.

Your example illuminates why pure democracy — the direct rule of the numerical majority — must always prove fatal to individual rights and minority interests. The cure lies not in abandoning popular government, but in so designing it that faction is made to check faction, and ambition to counteract ambition.
